title: Cat Unexplained Bruising on Abdomen: Urgent Triage Guide
meta_desc: Find out why unexplained bruising on your cat's belly is a medical emergency and how a photo can help your veterinarian triage the situation.
content_html:
TL;DR: Unexplained bruising on a cat's abdomen is a high-priority medical emergency indicating potential internal bleeding or clotting disorders that requires immediate veterinary attention.

What does unexplained bruising on my cat's abdomen mean?

Unexplained bruising, which may appear as purple splotches, tiny red dots (petechiae), or large discolored patches on the skin of the belly, indicates bleeding under the skin. Unlike humans, cats do not typically bruise from minor bumps because their fur and skin structure provide significant protection. Therefore, visible bruising often signals a serious internal issue, such as a blood clotting disorder, exposure to toxins like rodenticide, or severe internal trauma.

Is unexplained bruising on my cat a medical emergency?

  • The urgency level for unexplained bruising is High; you should seek immediate veterinary care at an emergency clinic.
  • Bruising is often the only visible sign of a life-threatening systemic problem or active internal bleeding.
  • Immediate care is especially critical if the bruising is accompanied by lethargy, pale gums, difficulty breathing, or a loss of appetite.

Why should I take a photo of the bruising to help with my cat's triage?

  • A clear, well-lit photo provides an invaluable timestamped baseline for the veterinary team.
  • Because bruising can shift, spread, or change in appearance very quickly, a photo allows the vet to see the original state of the site.
  • The image helps your veterinarian determine the speed and severity of the underlying condition while you are en route to the hospital.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats, unexplained bruising on the abdomen, particularly in the context of trauma or shock, necessitates immediate veterinary attention to rule out intra-abdominal hemorrhage. Common sources of abdominal bleeding include ruptured spleen or liver, avulsed mesenteric vessels, damaged muscle, or kidney avulsion. Signs of poor perfusion or shock are typically evident when significant blood loss (approximately 40 mL/kg) has occurred. Diagnostic tools such as radiographs, abdominocentesis, and abdominal ultrasound can aid in identifying smaller volumes of abdominal fluid. Exploratory laparotomy is indicated in cases of ongoing hemorrhage, inability to stabilize shock, organ compromise, diaphragmatic hernia, or evidence of organ rupture/peritonitis. In specific breeds such as Oriental shorthair and Siamese, liver lobe rupture secondary to amyloidosis should be considered, and ultrasonography with aspiration cytology or liver biopsy may be warranted to confirm the diagnosis.

Chapter: Emergency, Surgery, Gastroenterology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1670)

faqs.question: Could my cat have just bumped into something?
faqs.answer: While physical trauma can cause bruising, it is very rare for a cat to develop significant abdominal bruising from everyday activity. It is much more likely to be caused by a medical condition like thrombocytopenia (low platelets) or toxin ingestion.

faqs.question: What should I do while traveling to the vet?
faqs.answer: Keep your cat as calm and still as possible in a secure carrier. Avoid pressing on the abdomen, as this could exacerbate internal bleeding or cause pain.

faqs.question: What common toxins cause bruising in cats?
faqs.answer: The most common cause is anticoagulant rodenticides (rat poison). Even if you do not use these in your home, cats can be exposed by hunting rodents that have ingested the poison.
